coredata.dc:title: Vegetal route for synthesis of CQDs/CdS nanocomposites for photocatalytic reduction of CO2 to methanol under visible light
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Gawal, Pramod Madhukar
Golder, Animes Kumar
coredata.dc:description: The green pathway for the synthesis of nanoparticles targeting the photoreduction of CO2 to value-added chemicals is a potential approach to control industrial CO2 emission. In this study, we synthesized bio-based CdS(bio) nanorods using plant-based phytochemicals found in Aegle marmelos and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using orange peels. CQDs (7 nm) were homogeneously incorporated into CdS(bio) nanorods via simple deposition, forming CQDs/CdS(bio) nanocomposites. The catalysts were thoroughly characterized using diffraction, microscopic, spectroscopic, and electrochemical techniques. CQDs/CdS(bio) composites had a diameter and length of 73 nm and 822 nm with 82.25 m2/g specific surface area. CQDs/CdS(bio) composites showed a fourfold increase in both photocurrent density (0.38 μA/cm2) and CO2 adsorption capacity (0.292 mmol/g) compared to CdS(bio) nanorods alone. The conduction band of the composite (−0.92 eV) becomes more negative compared to CdS(bio) (−0.85 eV). Moreover, the composite formation notably improved decay time by 2.35 folds and reduced photoluminescence intensity by 59.23% compared to CdS(bio), indicating enhanced charge separation and reduced charge carrier recombination. Furthermore, the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/CdS(bio) nanocomposites was investigated for CO2 reduction to methanol under visible light (250 W, λ > 420 nm, 2.2 W/m2, 4.2719 ×1018 photons/m2.s) without any sacrificial reagent. The effect of the mass fraction of CQDs on CdS and catalyst loading on photocatalytic CO2 reduction has been investigated. The optimal CQDs/CdS(bio) loading (0.50% w/w) exhibited the maximum methanol yield of 1060.52 μmol/g·h (apparent quantum efficiency 7%) over 5 h. CQDs/CdS(bio) nanocomposites exhibited strong stability (test up to 25 h in five consecutive cycles), retaining the morphological (0.11% variation in size) and structural (4.2% variation in crystallinity index) attributes. This work would provide valuable insights into the development of bio-based CdS-based composites for efficient PCO2RR into valuable chemicals.

head.ce:title: Vegetal route for synthesis of CQDs/CdS nanocomposites for photocatalytic reduction of CO2 to methanol under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Guwahati, Assam 781039, India

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Guwahati, Assam-781039, INDIA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The green pathway for the synthesis of nanoparticles targeting the photoreduction of CO2 to value-added chemicals is a potential approach to control industrial CO2 emission. In this study, we synthesized bio-based CdS(bio) nanorods using plant-based phytochemicals found in Aegle marmelos and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using orange peels. CQDs (7 nm) were homogeneously incorporated into CdS(bio) nanorods via simple deposition, forming CQDs/CdS(bio) nanocomposites. The catalysts were thoroughly characterized using diffraction, microscopic, spectroscopic, and electrochemical techniques. CQDs/CdS(bio) composites had a diameter and length of 73 nm and 822 nm with 82.25 m2/g specific surface area. CQDs/CdS(bio) composites showed a fourfold increase in both photocurrent density (0.38 μA/cm2) and CO2 adsorption capacity (0.292 mmol/g) compared to CdS(bio) nanorods alone. The conduction band of the composite (−0.92 eV) becomes more negative compared to CdS(bio) (−0.85 eV). Moreover, the composite formation notably improved decay time by 2.35 folds and reduced photoluminescence intensity by 59.23% compared to CdS(bio), indicating enhanced charge separation and reduced charge carrier recombination. Furthermore, the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/CdS(bio) nanocomposites was investigated for CO2 reduction to methanol under visible light (250 W, λ > 420 nm, 2.2 W/m2, 4.2719 ×1018 photons/m2.s) without any sacrificial reagent. The effect of the mass fraction of CQDs on CdS and catalyst loading on photocatalytic CO2 reduction has been investigated. The optimal CQDs/CdS(bio) loading (0.50% w/w) exhibited the maximum methanol yield of 1060.52 μmol/g·h (apparent quantum efficiency 7%) over 5 h. CQDs/CdS(bio) nanocomposites exhibited strong stability (test up to 25 h in five consecutive cycles), retaining the morphological (0.11% variation in size) and structural (4.2% variation in crystallinity index) attributes. This work would provide valuable insights into the development of bio-based CdS-based composites for efficient PCO2RR into valuable chemicals.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The world is dependent on the burning of non-renewable fossil fuels to meet the bulk of its energy demand (∼80%) even in the 21st century [1]. Consequently, excessive CO2 emission causes the grievous greenhouse gas effect [2,3]. Currently, CO2 emission stands at over 40 gigatons per year with an atmospheric concentration of 424 ppm (International Energy Agency). In fact, global energy consumption has been estimated to rise by 28% in the next 17 years [4,5] because of the industrial boom and improved living standards of humans. Factually, the world needs sustainable technologies to fulfill the mammoth energy demand alongside CO2 mitigation to control the greenhouse gas effect.

The photoreduction of CO2 has been considered as one of the most efficient techniques for CO2 utilization because abundant solar energy and H2O are used in this process to convert CO2 into valuable chemicals such as ethanol (C2H5OH), methanol (CH3OH), formic acid (HCOOH), methane (CH4), carbon monoxide (CO), etc. [2,3,6,7–9]. This process could mimic natural photosynthesis in the plant [10,11]. Therefore, photocatalytic reduction of CO2 could meet the energy demand with a plausible solution to CO2 emission [12,13].

CO2 is a very stable linearly symmetric molecule with excellent thermodynamic stability. The double bond (CO) energy of CO2 is 750 kJ/mol compared to the single bond energy of 336 (C-C), 327 (C-O), and 411 kJ/mol (C-H) [14,15]. Due to the large negative potential of CO2 (−1.9 V vs. NHE, pH 7), the direct one-electron reduction of CO2 is challenging [16]. To date, no photocatalyst has been invented for a single electron for CO2 reduction. In contrast, the multielectron/proton-assisted reduction is an alternative and more beneficial approach because of the lower CO2 reduction potential [17,18]. Therefore, designing an active, robust, and cost-effective multifunctional photocatalyst is essential to achieve efficient CO2 reduction to useful chemicals for practical applications.

For photocatalytic CO2 reduction reaction, several nanocatalysts have been tested, namely, TiO2 [19], ZnO[20], ZnS [21], SiC [22], g-C3N4 [23], CdS [14], In2O3 [24], WO3 [25] , Bi2S3 [26], Cu2O [27], and MOFs[28]. Among these, CdS has been intensively studied for CO2 reduction owing to suitable bandgap (2.4 eV) and conduction band potential (−0.95 V vs. NHE, pH 7) and high solar energy conversion [13,14]. However, CdS suffers from oxidative corrosion and quick recombination of e-/h+ pairs [14]. As a remedial action, various modification techniques have been developed, namely, doping with metal and non-metal [29], morphological control [13], co-catalysis [6], and formation of heterojunction [30] and Z-scheme heterojunction[31].

The chemical synthesis methods of photocatalysts involve costly and environmentally aggressive chemicals. However, the bio-based approach for synthesizing semiconductor nanoparticles (NPs) has advantages over chemical-based methods, such as its eco-friendly nature, cost-effectiveness, one-pot synthesis process, and abundant sources of biomaterials[32,33]. Developing green pathways for NPS synthesis and their application in CO2 photoreduction to value-added chemicals could be a potential technique for controlling industrial CO2 emissions. The Northeastern states of India are the hub of several tropical and subtropical plants. These plants could be employed as potential capping and reducing analytes for the quick and effective synthesis of semiconductor nanomaterials[34]. A short exposure of microwave irradiation in concurrence with the bio-based process also could accelerate the rate of NPs formation by several folds.

The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained tremendous research interest owing to diverse applications in the last two decades. CQDs own unique characteristics, such as low toxicity, luminescence, and high thermal stability with zero-dimensionality (<10 nm size)[35,36]. In addition, due to their superior charge transfer and electron donor/ acceptor properties, CQDs have been considered promising candidates for developing effective and stable composite photocatalysts [37,38]. Different techniques have been used to synthesize CQDs using several carbon sources such as urea, thiourea, citric acid, ethylene glycol, etc. The renewable and abundant biomass reserves are also potential carbon precursors for CQDS synthesis. The biomass sources such as leaves, flowers, or roots of the plant [39,40], fruits and their peels [36], human derivatives [41], and animal derivatives [42] are carbonized for the low-cost and environmentally friendly synthesis of CQDs.

In this study, we synthesized microwave-assisted bio-based CdS(bio) nanorods using plant-based phytochemicals present in Aegle marmelos. It contains a variety of powerful reducing and capping analytes, including alkaloids, flavonoids, phenol, tannins, and saponins [34,43,44]. CdS(bio) nanorods were modified to minimize their oxidative corrosion and high electron/hole recombination during photocatalytic CO2 reduction reaction (PCO2RR). Further, bio-based CQDs were synthesized from waste orange peels and incorporated into CdS(bio) by a simple deposition method. The modified catalysts (CQDs/CdS(bio) composites) were tested for PCO2RR to methanol formation in batch mode under visible-light irradiation. The maximum methanol production yield of 1062.68 μmol/g·h was achieved using CQDs/CdS(bio) composites with CQDs loading of 0.50% (w/w). The reusability of CQDs/CdS(bio) composites also has been examined. Factually, this is the first study on the bio-based synthesis of composites nano-structures for PCO2RR to methanol formation.

Cadmium chloride monohydrate (99.50%) was obtained from Himedia, India. Sodium sulfide (50%), ethanol (99.99%), sodium sulfate anhydrous (99%), and dimethyl sulfoxide (99.9%) were supplied by Merck, India. Methanol (99.8%) was obtained from Sigma Aldrich, Germany. Nafion® D-521 dispersion solution was obtained from Alfa Aesar, USA. Deuterium oxide (99.9%) was procured from Sigma Aldrich, USA. Carbon dioxide (99.9%) and nitrogen (99.9%) were provided by Assam Air Product Pvt. Ltd., India. Distilled water from Millipore (Elix-3Milli-q, USA) was used to conduct all experimental works.

Fresh Aegle marmelos leaves (5 g) were collected and washed thoroughly under running water for 10 min to eliminate dust particles, and then a final wash using distilled water. The leaves were placed in 50 mL of 50% ethanol and stirred for 20 min at 450 rpm on a magnetic stirrer at 70 °C until the solvent turned dark green [45]. The extract at room temperature was filtered via a nylon filter and stored for further use. The previously reported methodology followed for the quantitative assay of phytochemicals contents in Aegle marmelos [43,46] is presented in Table S1 of the Supplementary Information. The analytes present in Aegle marmelos are identified by HRMS (Fig. S1 of Supplementary Information) and summarized in Table S2 . A flavonoid fraction of 3,5-dihydroxy-6-methyl-2 H-pyran-4(3 H)-one was rich in the bio-extract, and it could act as a reducing and capping agent to avoid aggregation of CdS(bio) nanorods. For the synthesis of CdS nanorods, a uniform flavonoid content of 22.63 mg/g was maintained in bio-extract across different batches of experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CdS(bio) nanorods using bio-extract
ce:section.ce:para: Aegle marmelos extract with a volume of 1 mL was added dropwise to 10 mL solution of CdCl2·H2O (0.1 M). Under stirring, add 10 mL of Na2S (0.1 M) dropwise to the CdCl2 solution [47]. The resulting solution was transferred into a microwave reactor (Discover System, CEM Matthews NC, USA) and irradiated for 5 min at 100 °C. The precipitate was centrifuged (5000 rpm, 5 min) and washed numerous times using distilled water and 50% (v/v) ethanol. The obtained yellow residue was oven-dried for 12 h at 65 °C to recover CdS(bio) nanorods. The control synthesis of CdS(con) was done at the experimental condition (optimized using bio-extract) using distilled water instead of bio-extract. The schematic of CdS(bio) nanorod synthesis is given in Fig. S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs using waste orange peels
ce:section.ce:para: Orange peels were purchased from the fruit market located at IIT Guwahati. Firstly, orange peels were thoroughly washed in tap water, dried in sunlight, and then ground to powder (20–40 µm, Fig. S3) using a grinder-mixer. A quantity of 1 g of obtained powder was mixed with 40 mL distilled water, and the mixture was stirred for 15 min to obtain a homogeneous suspension. Then, the solution was placed in a Teflon-line hydrothermal reactor and heated at 180 °C for 12 h [38]. After reaching room temperature, the solution was centrifuged (5000 rpm, 10 min) to separate residues and then filtered using a 0.2 µm PTFE filter. The CQDs residue (10 mg/mL) was stored for further use. The primary constituents of orange peel powder were determined by CHNSO analysis to be carbon (43%), hydrogen (5%), nitrogen (3%), sulfur (0%), and oxygen (49%). For the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a uniform carbon content of 43% (w/w) was maintained across different batches of experiments.

A facile deposition method was used to prepare CQDs/CdS(bio) nanocomposites ( Fig. 1). Firstly, a known quantity of CQDs (0.25%, 0.50%, 0.75%, and 1% w/w) was mixed in 50 mL aliquot using distilled water in a beaker. Then, 100 mg CdS(bio) nanorods were added to this dispersion, and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 12 h. The solution was then centrifuged and left to dry overnight in an oven [37].

The crystalline nature of synthesized catalysts was tested using a powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Smart lab, Rigaku, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.540 Å) in 2θ between 5° and 80°. The valence state and elemental composition of synthesized catalysts were examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI 5000 Versa Probe III, ULVAC-PHI, USA) with an Al Kα (1486.6 eV) as an X-ray source (1486.6 eV). XPSPEAK41 software was employed to produce the XPS spectra. The bandgap of catalysts was calculated using UV-Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV-DRS) (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan) recorded against BaSO4 as a reference. The particle morphology was investigated using a Field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Sigma 300, Zeiss, Germany). The particle sizes of catalysts and their morphology were analysed by field emission transmission electron microscope (FETEM, JEM2100F, JEOL, Japan). The specific area of the catalyst was calculated using a BET analyzer (Micromeritics, Tristar-II 3020, USA) by investigating the N2-adsorption-desorption isotherm in the 0.1 to 0.9 relative pressure (P/P0) range. CO2 adsorption was tested at 0.1–1 bar using a high-pressure gas sorption analyzer (SorbHP1-XKRLSPN100, Quantachrome Instrument, Austria). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, I.R. Affinity 1, Shimadzu, Japan) investigated the functional groups. The photoluminescence (PL) of catalyst particles was studied at 360 nm excitation wavelength (Fluromax-4, Horiba Jobin Yvon, Japan). To comprehend the charge carrier dynamic, time-resolved PL (TRPL) spectra were recorded at 405 nm excitation and 450 nm emission wavelengths (Lifespec II, Eddinburg Instrument, UK). The mass spectra of Aegle marmelos leave extract were obtained using a high-resolution mass spectrometer (HRMS, G1969A, Agilent, USA) in positive ion mode. Dynamic light scattering (DLS, Litesizer™ 500, Anton Paar, USA) was used to determine the hydrodynamic diameter of the catalyst particles and the surface charge characteristic of the catalyst by zeta potential. The carbon content of orange peel powder was measured using a CHNSO analyzer (Vario MACRO Cube, Elementar, India).

Visible light-assisted PCO2RR was performed in a flat bottom quartz reactor (50 mL) with a closed headspace using a monochromatic 250 W mercury vapour lamp (λ = 420 nm) (Lelesil innovative system, India) as a light source at room temperature under constant magnetic stirring. The overall PCO2RR tests were conducted at a light intensity of 2.2 W/m2, with a photon flux of 4.2719 × 1018 photons/m2.s. A known amount of catalyst (20 mg) was added to 20 mL distilled water in a typical experiment. The suspension underwent a 15 min sonication to avoid particle agglomeration before the reaction. Before each experiment, dissolved gases were removed by passing 20 mL/min of N2 (99.99%) gas for 15 min. After that, CO2 gas (99.99% purity) was bubbled at a 10 mL/min rate through the solution for 30 min under constant stirring to reach CO2 saturation (pH 5.7–5.8). CO2 concentration was measured using an Ion-selective electrode (PS-3517, Thermo Scientific, USA). The light was irradiated afterward, and a 1.2 mL sample was withdrawn at different time intervals for NMR analysis (ASCEND 600, Bruker, Germany). The methanol standard was used to develop the calibration (2–10 mM) to measure its concentration. A linear calibration fit was obtained with R2 of 0.999. The H1 NMR graph of methanol is presented in Fig. S4.
The following equation (Eq. 1) was used to calculate methanol formation's apparent quantum efficiency (AQE).

The photoelectrochemical properties of catalysts were investigated using a three-electrode Potentiostat/Galvanostat system (AUTOLAB 302 N, Metrohm Autolab B.V, Netherlands). A working electrode of photocatalyst-coated FTO glass, a counter electrode made of platinum wire, and the reference electrode made of Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) were all employed for this test with 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte solution[48]. 20 mg catalyst was suspended in 400 μL Nafion and ethanol (1:3 v/v) solution. This dispersion was subjected to sonication for 30 min to form a uniform mixture. The working electrode was prepared by spin coating catalyst/Nafion mixture (SpinNXG-P1, Apex Instruments, Kolkata, India) with a photocatalytic area (projected) of 1 cm2 at 2000 rpm for 20 s onto the FTO glass and dried in the oven overnight. The photocurrents were measured by illuminating a 250 W mercury vapor lamp. Electrochemical impedance studies were performed at 10−2 and 105 Hz frequencies range within the 0–1.5 V potential range (vs. Ag/AgCl). The electrolyte solution was degassed by N2 purging before all tests.

X-ray diffraction:
Fig. 2 depicts the XRD patterns of CdS(bio) and CQDs/CdS(bio) composites. For CdS(bio), the diffraction peaks observed at 2θ values of 26.31, 43.64, and 51.68° correspond to the crystal plane with miller indices of (111), (220), and (311), respectively. It confirms the cubic CdS phase (JCPDF database, 01-083-5247). The peak intensity and its position well agreed with the earlier study [49]. The peak position of CQDs/CdS(bio) composites is in accordance with CdS(bio). It implies that the incorporation of CQDs(bio) during the formation of CQDs/CdS(bio) composites didn’t change its crystal structure or phase. The XRD peak of CQDs was absent in the composites structure because of its tiny mass loading (<1% w/w), well dispersity, and amorphous nature [37]. The crystallinity index of catalysts was computed using the equation (Eq. 2) shown below [50].
(2)
Crystallinity
index
%
=
A c
A t
× 100

Where Ac is the area of prominent crystalline peaks [(111), (220), (311)], and At is the total area under the peaks. The crystallinity index of CdS(bio) and 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) were found to be 70.20% and 69.9%, respectively.

XPS analysis:
Fig. 3 depicts the survey and high-resolution (HR) spectra of CdS(bio) and CQDs/CdS(bio) composites. Fig. 3b shows HR Cd 3d spectra of CdS(bio) with two distinct peaks at 409.26 and 402.5 eV ascribing to the Cd 3d3/2 and Cd 3d5/2 spin-orbits, respectively, owing to Cd2+ in CdS(bio) nanorods [10]. The S 2p spectra (Fig. 3c) of CdS(bio) showed two peaks at 160.1 and 158.88 eV. It is attributed to the S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 with a splitting energy of 1.2 eV, which indicates the S2- valence state in CdS(bio) nanorods [51]. The HR S 2p and Cd 3d spectra of 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) composites also showed a similar trend. The S 2p and Cd 3d peak positions were shifted towards the higher binding energy. A reduction in the electron density of CdS(bio) is suggested by the positive shift in the binding energy peak of S 2p and Cd 3d. It implies that CdS and CQDs have a strong chemical interaction that facilitates the transport of electrons from CdS to CQDs [37]. The HR of C 1s spectra (Fig. 3d) of 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) composites (binding energy peak at 284.8 eV) correspond to the C­C/CC of the graphitic or aliphatic structure of CQDs on the surface of CdS. The 288.07 and 286.3 eV peaks correspond to CO and C-O, respectively [52]. The HR O 1s spectra of 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) composites exhibited three major deconvoluted peaks at 533.3, 532.7, and 531.4 eV (Fig. 3e) corresponding to the C-OH/C-O-Cs, CO/Cd(OH)2/Cd-O, and C-O/OH- respectively [37,38]. Due to the hydroxide group’s structural compatibility with sulphur, it may easily interact with the CdS lattice, forming CdO or Cd(OH)2 [29,38].

UV–vis spectra: The UV-Vis spectra of CdS(bio), CQDs(bio), and CQDs/CdS(bio) composites are shown in Fig. 4a. CdS(bio) showed a significant absorption threshold at 520 nm. The absorption edge of CQDs/CdS(bio) composites shifted towards longer wavelengths, resulting in a redshift. The absorption spectra of CQDs (Fig. S5) revealed two peaks at 280 nm and 220 nm corresponding to n-π * (CO) and π-π * (CC) transitions, respectively. The delocalized large π bonds were present, which could improve the light absorption intensity [53,54]. The bandgap was computed by using Tauc’s relation (Eq. 3) [55].
(3)

( hv −
E

)
n
hv
Where α = F(R) denotes the absorption coefficient, hν is the photon energy, Eg is the optical bandgap energy, n is a constant, and its value of ½ represents the direct allowed transition [54]. Fig. 4b displays the plot of (αhν)1/2 vs. hν. CdS(bio) and 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) bandgap were determined to be 2.42 and 2.32 eV, respectively. The results agree with the values reported earlier [37]. The bandgap of 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) composites is slightly reduced compared to CdS(bio) due to the quantum confinement effect of CQDs[56]. A similar reduction of bandgap was observed with g-C3N4 (2.7 eV to 2.62 eV) [57] and Cu2O (2.0 to 1.91 eV) [58] with the incorporation of CQDs.

Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) spectra: The conduction band position of photocatalyst specimens is a significant aspect in determining the ability of CO2 reduction. The valence band maximum (VBM) of the synthesized catalyst was characterized using UPS, and outcomes are shown in Fig. 5a. The linear extrapolation approach is used to calculate the VBM of each sample, which is determined to be 1.57 and 1.40 eV for CdS(bio) and 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) composites, respectively. Based on the results of UPS spectra and UV–vis DRS spectra (bandgap), the conduction band maximum (CBM) was calculated using the relation, ECB =EVB-Eg [3] . CBM of CdS(bio) and 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) are found to be − 0.85 and − 0.92 eV, respectively [3,29,37]. Fig. 5b depicts the band diagram of CdS(bio) and 0.50%(w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) composites. It is clear that the CQDs/CdS(bio) composites exhibit a favorable CBM potential for an increased methanol production rate.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra: The PL spectra were collected at a 360 nm wavelength of excitation to understand the charge carrier dynamic and its trapping efficiency of synthesized materials ( Fig. 6). The prominent peak observed at 450 nm suggests fast recombination of e-/h+ pairs. CQDs/CdS(bio) composites exhibited substantially lower PL intensities than that of CdS(bio) (Fig. 6a) due to the presence of delocalized π-conjugated structure in CQDs, which has an excellent electron-conducting characteristic [59]. It indicates a better separation of photoexcited charge carriers and reduced recombination of e-/h+ pairs, allowing a rapid transfer of electrons from the conduction band (CB) of CdS to CQDs [29,37,60]. Hence, more electrons could be available for the CO2 reduction reactions. A similar observation was reported for CQDs/g-C3N4 [54] and CQDs/Cu2O composites. Furthermore, CQDs exhibited a prominent peak at 443 nm with 360 nm excitation wavelength with a blue emission spectrum (Fig. 6b), suggesting a typical fluorescent nature of CQDs [35]. The strong electron binding is due to the combined effect of the quantum (blue fluorescent) phenomenon and the conjugation effect arising from CQDs' delocalized π bond [53]. The CQDs(bio) showed a dark yellow and blue fluorescent when exposed to visible light and ultraviolet (UV) light, respectively (inset of Fig. 6b).

Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL): TRPL spectra ( Fig. 7) of CdS(bio) and CQDs/CdS(bio) composites were recorded with 1000 counts at 405 nm and 450 nm of excitation and emission wavelengths, respectively, to understand the charge transfer behavior. The fitted parameter is shown in Table 1 . A bi-exponential decay function was used to fit the TRPL decay curves. The average lifetime (τavg) was estimated using (Eq. 4) [57].

Where A1, A2 represents amplitudes, and τ1, τ2 represents the lifetime of the charge carriers. The average lifetime of charge carriers in CdS(bio) and 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) composites were determined to be 1.59 ns and 3.74 ns, respectively. This indicates that the 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) exhibited better e-/h+ pairs separation efficiency compared to the CdS(bio). Incorporating CQDs onto CdS serves as a reservoir for capturing excited electrons facilitated by the surface junction formed between CdS and CQDs. Consequently, this enhanced the separation of e-/h+ pairs and reduced their recombination[61,62].

Electrochemical attributes of CQDs/CdS(bio) composites: The charge separation efficiency of CdS(bio) and 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) composites were examined using the transient photocurrent measurement and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS). The chronoamperometry test (
Fig. 8a) was carried out with an interval of 25 s of the light on/off cycle. The average photocurrent density of 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) composites is 0.38 μA/cm2, which is nearly four times greater than CdS(bio) (0.095 μA/cm2). It indicates that photogenerated e-/h+ pairs are separated efficiently with CQDs incorporation onto CdS(bio) nanorods [6].

EIS spectra of CdS(bio) nanorods and 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) composites nanorods under both dark conditions and light irradiation are presented in Fig. 8b. Under visible light, the arc radius of 0.5 (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) composites was reduced, indicating a higher charge separation and transfer efficiency. It implies that CQDs incorporation could change the distribution of charges and facilitate charge transfer of CdS(bio) nanorods [37]. Furthermore, increased capacitance indicates improved CQDs storing characteristics on the photoanode/electrolyte [29,63]. A similar observation was reported for CQDs/Cu2O composites and CQDs/g-C3N4 [57,64].

Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra: FTIR spectra of CdS(bio), CQDs(bio), and CQDs/CdS(bio) composites are shown in Fig. 9. The strong broadband peak at around 3290 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of O-H bonds in water molecules[65]. The peak at 1572 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of CC cyclic alkene and at 1405 cm−1 to the bending vibration C-C in aromatic compounds. The weak absorption band at 1030 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of C-O bonds, and 616 cm−1 corresponds to the Cd-S stretch vibration. An additional peak corresponds to the CdS in CQDs/CdS(bio) composites due to the induction of CQDs into CdS [29]. A weak absorption band at about 2930 cm−1 in both CdS(bio) and CQDs/CdS(bio) composites corresponding to the Cd-O stretching vibration [38] was also confirmed by XPS analysis (Fig. 9a). A broad absorption band at 3441 cm−1 indicates the stretching vibration of O-H bonds (Fig. 9b). The peak at 1640 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the CO bond. The 1405 and 1070 cm−1 peaks correspond to the bending vibration of COO- and C-O/C-O-C bonds, respectively [66]. The presence of the –OH group on CQDs indicates that it can donate electrons in PCO2RR [67].

Zeta potential: The zeta potential of a sample (catalyst + DI water) was measured at different pH (2−12) using the dynamic light scattering method. The variation of zeta potential (mV) against pH is shown in Fig. S6 of the Supplementary Information. The pH at the zero-point charge (pHzpc) is found to be 5.61 which is close to the CO2 saturation point in the aqueous media.

FESEM and FETEM analyses:
Fig. 10 shows the FESEM images of CdS(bio), CdS(con), and 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) composites. CdS(bio) nanorods with a defined shape were successfully formed in the proposed bio-based route (Fig. 10a). Whereas no defined structural formation of CdS nanoparticles was noted for CdS(con) (Fig. 10b). It is evident that CQDs incorporation (CQDs/CdS(bio)) didn’t cause any morphological alteration (Fig. 10c). The average length and diameter of both CdS(bio) and 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) nanorods were 822 and 73 nm [68,69].

Fig. 11 shows the FETEM of CdS(bio), CQDs(bio), and CQDs/CdS(bio) composites. The average diameter of nanorods was 822 nm, which is consistent with FESEM analyses (Figs. 11a and 11b). The average CQDs size was 7 nm (Fig. 11c). Fig. 11d shows the HRTEM images of 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) composites. The interplanar spacing of CdS(bio) lattice fringes is 0.34 nm, corresponding to the (111) plane [70]. It was 0.32 nm for (002) graphitic lattice planes of CQDs [39]. The SAED patterns of 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) composites (Fig. 11d(insect)) exhibited the amorphous nature of CQDs compared to CdS(bio), which has a monocrystalline structure with one-dimensional morphology. The well-crystalline phase of CdS nanorods was indicated by SAED patterns of CdS(bio) nanorods (Fig. S7 of Supplementary Information) [71], but the amorphous nature of CQDs resulted in a fuzzy ring structure in the SAED patterns of CQDs [40]. The SAED pattern of 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) composites showed a slight SAED pattern deviation. It could be attributed to CQDs incorporation onto CdS(bio) nanorods. EDX analysis of 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) composites (Fig. 11e) showed C, S, and Cd contents of 20.14%, 16.29%, and 63.57%, respectively. Fig. 11f shows the elemental mapping of the element present in the heterostructures.

BET surface area analysis: The surface area and pore structure of CdS(bio) and 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) were determined using N2 adsorption-desorption, and the results are summarized in Table 1. Fig. 12 illustrates the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and corresponding pore size distribution curve (inset of Fig. 12). Both CdS(bio) and 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) composites exhibited type IV isotherms with mesoporous nature with an average pore size was 7.58 and 6.48 nm, respectively. Hence, CQDs/CdS(bio) composites could provide effective transport pathways for reactant and product molecules[72,73]. Almost no variation was noted in surface area and adsorption isotherm with CQDs incorporation onto CdS(bio). As seen from Table 2, 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) composites exhibit higher CO2 adsorption capacity (0.292 mmol/g) than that of CdS s (0.075 mmol/g) tested at 1 bar. The π–π-conjugate structure of CQDs enhances CO2 adsorption. CO2 molecules have delocalized π-conjugate binding π3 and π-π combination interaction between CO2 and CQDs for enhanced CO2 adsorption onto CQDs/CdS(bio) composites [6,74].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Testing of catalytic activity for PCO2RR
ce:section.ce:para: The production of methanol using synthesized catalysts was tested under visible light illumination. The methanol standard calibration graph is given in Fig. S8 . There was no noticeable methanol production in the absence of either catalyst or light irradiation. The control experiment was performed using composite and water without CO2 purging under visible light irradiation. The NMR spectra, depicted in Fig. S9, confirm that methanol is produced from the CO2 feedstock in the system.

Fig. 13 a shows the methanol production rate using CdS(bio) and CQDs/CdS(bio) composites (0.25%, 0.50%, 0.75%, and 1% w/w). In fact, methanol production was negligible using CdS(bio) due to its photo-corrosion (Fig. S10c & S11a) and quick recombination of e-/h+ pairs. It implies that bare CdS is not a suitable catalyst for reducing CO2 to methanol [6,75]. For CQDs, there is no product formation (Fig. S12) due to a lack of effective catalytic sites [37]. In the case of CQDs/CdS(bio) composites, the rate of methanol formation exhibited an increasing trend with a loading percentage of CQDs and reached its maximum at 0.50% (w/w) loading of CQDs with a yield of 773.38 μmol/g·h. The rate of methanol formation was then decreased drastically with an increase in the percentage (w/w) of CQDs. An excess CQDs loading would lead to particle agglomeration and block the active sites of CdS(bio), hindering light absorption [29,37,38]. The hydrodynamic diameter of the catalysts was determined by DLS, and the corresponding result can be found in Table S3. The particle size was increased significantly with the increase in CQDs loading onto CdS(bio) beyond 0.50% (w/w). It indicates that an excess CQDs loading would lead to particle agglomeration. Therefore, 0.50% (w/w) CQDs loading was considered the optimal CQDs loading.

The methanol production rate with different photocatalyst concentrations (0, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1 g/L) of 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) composites is presented in Fig. 13b. A concentration of 0.50 g/L photocatalyst exhibited the highest methanol production, yielding 1062.68 μmol/g·h and AQE of 7% (calculation of AQE provided in Table S4). The solution becomes turbid, preventing light irradiation at increasing concentrations of photocatalysts [38]. The methanol production rate achieved in this study was compared with values reported in the literature (Table S5), implying a clear superiority of bio-based CQDs/CdS(bio) composites.

The time-dependent PCO2RR to methanol production over 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) composites was tested with five repeated cycles to investigate the functional stability of the synthesized catalyst (Fig. 13c). After 25 h reaction, the composites maintained a consistent photocatalytic activity with 1.28% decline in their performance in the 5 the cycle due to the inevitable loss of photocatalyst during the multistep recovery processes. The excellent stability of the composites suggests that the incorporation of CQDs on the surface of CdS(bio) reduced its photocorrosion and suppressed e-/h+ pairs recombination [29,37,38].

The impact of common inorganic ions on the photocatalytic CO2 reduction to methanol in an aqueous media under visible light was investigated, by mimicking the real environmental condition. We selected the average ion concentration as reported in the literature[6]. The results are summarized in Table. S6 of Supplementary Information. Ca2+ and Mg2+ cations exhibited a slight increase in CO2 reduction due to improved CO2 adsorption. While the influence of Na+ and K+ was minimal. For anions, Cl− addition showed a negligible impact, but SO4 2− hindered CO2 reduction. However, CO3 2− could facilitate the CO2 conversion reaction by serving as a carbon source for methanol formation.

Fig. S10 illustrates the XPS analysis of CdS(bio/used) and 0.50% (w/w) of CQDs/CdS(bio/used) composites. The survey spectrum (Fig. S10a) validates the existence of Cd, S, C and O in the composites . Only Cd is present in the CdS(bio/used) specimen as expected. The high-resolution spectra of 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) (Figs. S10b-S10e) confirm consistent peak intensities before and after PCO2RR. However, in the case of CdS(bio/used) (Figs. S10b-S10c), the S 2p peak intensity is shifted to 168.64 eV, indicating sulfur oxidation (photocorrosion) and formation of SO4 2- [76].

Fig. S11 shows the FESEM images of CdS(bio/used) and 0.50% (w/w) of CQDs/CdS(bio/used) composites after PCO2RR. CdS(bio) nanorods underwent significant morphological changes due to potential photocorrosion. However, no changes were noted for the composites [77]. The XRD spectra after PCO2RR of CdS(bio/used) and 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio/used) composites are shown in Fig. S13. The morphological and structural characteristic parameters of CdS(bio/used) and 0.50%(w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio/used) before and after the PCO2RR are mentioned in Table S7 . A reduction in crystallinity index of 15% was observed for CdS(bio) against 47.74% and 39.83% reduction in its length and diameter. In comparison, it was only 0.057% and 0.80% for CQDs/CdS(bio/used) composites, respectively.

This study was successful in synthesizing CQDs/CdS nanorods composites in a bio-based route (diameter 73 nm, length 822 nm, surface area 82.25 m2/g, bandgap 2.32 eV) by using natural precursors, namely, orange peels and phytochemicals present in Aegle marmelos vegetal extract. The photocatalytic activity of CQDs/CdS(bio) composites was evaluated for CO2 reduction to methanol under 250 W visible light(λ > 420 nm, 4.2719 ×1018 photons/m2.s). The bandgap of CdS(bio) was reduced from 2.42 to 2.32 eV with the incorporation of CQDs (0.50% w/w) onto CdS(bio). The decay lifetime (3.74 ns) of CQDs/CdS(bio) composites was 2.35 folds higher than CdS(bio). CQDs/CdS(bio) composites showed a strong affinity for CO2 adsorption (0.292 mmol/g at 1 bar) over CdS(bio) (0.075 mmol/g). The photocurrent density of 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) was 4 times higher than CdS(bio) nanorods. A remarkable methanol production rate of 1060.52 μmol/g·h (AQE 7%) was achieved using 0.50% (w/w) CQDs/CdS(bio) composites without using any sacrificial agent over 5 h irradiation. CQDs/CdS(bio) composites exhibited robust stability (tested for 25 h), with minimal morphological (0.11% size variation) and structural (4.2% crystallinity index variation) changes and resistant to photo and oxidative corrosion, whereas CdS(bio) underwent quick morphological and structural changes. In a nutshell, the present work would provide new insights for developing bio-based CdS-based composites for efficient PCO2RR to useful chemicals.
